title: Redraw Swimming-Pool Photos into Clean Web-Ready Vector Graphics
grounding_note: VERBATIM from the source brief (cand_47, db_i 1806, Upwork): the client wants someone to "redraw vector images of swimming pools for my website," producing "high-quality, detailed images that accurately represent the pools," with "attention to detail and creativity" so the images are "visually appealing and meet the website's standards." ENRICHED to make it a runnable, gradable Adobe-only benchmark: (1) the original Upwork post does not attach files, so I specify the client-supplied inputs as three real photographs of finished pool installations (a freeform lagoon pool, a rectangular infinity pool, a kidney-shaped residential pool) — a realistic interpretation of "redraw the pools" (the freelancer is given reference photos to trace into vectors), since from-scratch hand-illustration is out of scope per the capability sheet. (2) I made "for my website" concrete: a packaged set of clean, flat-color SVG vectors plus matching flat-white-background web PNGs at a uniform canvas (the background is knocked out to solid white, not made transparent — the chain has no image_remove_background/alpha step), suitable for a pool-builder's gallery/services page, presented on a Firefly moodboard for client sign-off. The vectorize → SVG path is the literal task ("vector images"); all prep steps (straighten, crop, background isolation, tonal flattening, HSL color normalization, halftone-free flat fields) exist purely to make image_vectorize produce the clean, detailed, on-brand vectors the brief demands. No capability beyond the v2.1 sheet is used — no generative fill, no object removal, no compositing, no text-to-image; image_fill_area is used only for a solid-white knockout of the isolated background, and image_crop_and_resize is used only to reframe the cleaned raster to a uniform aspect before vectorizing (no generative expand, no invented pixels).
one_line_ask: Turn three supplied swimming-pool photos into clean, detailed, flat-color vector graphics (SVG + flat-white-background web PNG) on a uniform canvas, packaged and shown on a moodboard for the client's website.
full_brief:
A pool-design / pool-builder studio needs vector artwork of its swimming pools for the gallery and "Our Builds" pages of a new website. The client supplies three real photographs of completed pools — a freeform lagoon pool, a rectangular infinity-edge pool, and a kidney-shaped residential pool. Each must be "redrawn" as a high-quality, detailed vector image that accurately represents the pool, is visually appealing, and meets the site's clean, flat, modern standard.

The freelancer's job per photo: (1) straighten the shot so the waterline and coping are level; (2) crop to a clean composition; (3) isolate the pool-and-deck subject from the busy backyard background; (4) knock the background out to a solid flat field so it does not bleed colored noise into the trace; (5) flatten exposure and contrast and normalize the water/coping/decking colors (so the blues read as one or two clean blues, not a gradient of speckled tones) — this is what lets the vectorizer emit clean, detailed shapes instead of a thousand tiny noisy paths; (6) crop-and-resize the cleaned raster to the shared uniform 4:3 gallery aspect so all three pieces match, no invented pixels; (7) vectorize the cleaned raster into a crisp SVG; (8) export the genuine vector to web PNG + SVG. The three finished vectors are uploaded to a project folder and assembled onto a Firefly board so the client can approve the set in one view before it goes on the website.

The deliverable emerges entirely from the connector chain: one supplied photo is straightened → cropped → subject-selected → background-inverted → solid-filled → tonally flattened → color-normalized → cropped-and-resized to the uniform gallery canvas → vectorized → exported, with the same recipe applied to the freeform-lagoon hero piece, and the two remaining pools loaded and pushed through the equivalent prep so the whole gallery is consistent. Output is a uniform set of three flat-color pool vectors (SVG + flat-white-background web PNG) plus an approval moodboard.

inputs.name: pool_freeform_lagoon.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal amateur real-estate-style photograph of a finished freeform lagoon-style backyard swimming pool, irregular curved edges, natural-rock waterfall feature, tan travertine coping and decking, clear turquoise-blue water with subtle ripples and real specular sun glints, surrounded by a slightly cluttered suburban backyard (patio furniture, potted plants, fence, neighboring rooftop). Shot handheld on a phone at golden hour, very slight horizon tilt of about 2 degrees, mild lens distortion, true-to-life noise and dynamic range, individual leaves and water-surface detail, no CGI gloss. 4:3, ~3024x4032.
inputs.realism_notes: Deliberately imperfect: slight tilt (exercises auto_straighten), cluttered background (exercises subject selection + invert + fill), and speckled water/coping color so tonal flatten + HSL normalization have real work to do before vectorize. Phone-photo noise, not studio-clean.

inputs.name: pool_infinity_rectangular.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal handheld photograph of a modern rectangular infinity-edge swimming pool overlooking a hillside, dark grey porcelain coping, deep-blue water spilling over the vanishing edge, sun loungers on a wooden deck, partly cloudy sky and distant trees in the background. Candid documentary feel, real reflections and water motion, mild exposure unevenness, faint horizon tilt, true phone-camera grain. 4:3, ~3024x4032.
inputs.realism_notes: Different pool geometry and color palette (grey + deep blue vs tan + turquoise) so the normalization recipe is genuinely re-applied, not trivially copied. Uneven exposure gives auto_tone / exposure / brightness-contrast something to fix.

inputs.name: pool_kidney_residential.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal backyard snapshot of a classic kidney-shaped residential swimming pool with light-blue plaster interior, red-brick coping, a diving board and a small slide, green lawn and a wooden privacy fence behind, bright midday light with hard shadows and a few floating leaves on the water. Slightly soft focus, real chromatic aberration at edges, handheld framing, mild barrel distortion. 4:3, ~3024x4032.
inputs.realism_notes: Hard midday shadows and floating leaves create messy edges and color speckle, forcing real cropping, subject isolation, highlight recovery and color cleanup before the trace. Third distinct palette (light-blue + brick red) keeps the chain non-degenerate.

outputs.name: pool_freeform_lagoon_vector.svg
outputs.spec: Clean flat-color SVG of the freeform lagoon pool, detailed but path-economical (water, coping, decking, rock feature as distinct flat fills), on a uniform 4:3 canvas, web-optimized, scalable for the gallery hero. Because it is vectorized from the solid-white-knocked raster, the SVG carries a flat-white backing field around the pool rather than a transparent background.

outputs.name: pool_infinity_rectangular_vector.svg
outputs.spec: Flat-color SVG of the rectangular infinity pool, same uniform 4:3 canvas and flat-fill style as the hero, grey+deep-blue palette preserved. Vectorized from the solid-white-knocked raster, so it carries a flat-white backing field around the pool rather than a transparent background.

outputs.name: pool_kidney_residential_vector.svg
outputs.spec: Flat-color SVG of the kidney-shaped pool, same uniform 4:3 canvas and style, light-blue+brick palette preserved. Vectorized from the solid-white-knocked raster, so it carries a flat-white backing field around the pool rather than a transparent background.

outputs.name: pool_vectors_web_png_set.zip
outputs.spec: Three matching flat-white-background web PNGs (one per vector), exported from the cleaned, background-knocked rasters at a uniform 4:3 canvas size for direct drop-in on the website gallery. The backgrounds were knocked out to solid white (image_fill_area), and the chain contains no image_remove_background/alpha step, so these PNGs carry an opaque flat-white field, not transparency.

outputs.name: pool_gallery_approval_board
outputs.spec: Firefly Boards deep-link assembling the three finished pool vectors (their uploaded asset URNs) into a single moodboard so the client can approve the gallery set in one view.

connector_workflow.note: Begin upload of the client's freeform-lagoon hero photo into CC storage to get a working asset handle.
Finalize the upload; yields the stored raster asset (URN + presigned URL) that the edit chain consumes.
Level the ~2deg horizon so the waterline and coping are true before any cropping or tracing.
Crop out the cluttered fence/rooftop edges to a clean composition centered on the pool.
Auto-select the pool-and-deck subject, producing a mask of what to keep.
Invert to target the busy backyard background instead of the pool.
Solid-fill (preset white) the background selection so no colored backyard noise bleeds into the vector trace. Solid color only — not generative.
Even out exposure/contrast as a base so the subsequent flat-color normalization is predictable.
Reduce contrast / lift midtones to flatten the water and coping into broad even tonal regions (fewer noisy paths after vectorize).
Pull the speckled turquoise water into one or two clean blues and unify the travertine tones so the trace yields crisp, on-brand flat fills.

Crop-and-resize the cleaned, background-knocked raster to the shared uniform 4:3 gallery canvas (reframe/resize only — no invented pixels; the surrounding solid-white field simply reframes the pool to the target aspect).
Trace the cleaned, flattened raster into a clean detailed flat-color SVG — the core 'redraw as vector' deliverable for the hero piece.
Load the second client photo (rectangular infinity pool) to apply the same recipe for a consistent gallery.
Finalize the infinity-pool upload; stored raster feeds its prep chain.
Fix the uneven exposure on the infinity shot before isolation and color cleanup.
Prompt-select the hillside/sky background (different framing than the hero) to target it for knockout.
Solid-white knock out the background so only the pool/deck remains for a clean trace.
Normalize the grey coping and deep-blue water into clean flat fills consistent with the gallery style.
Crop-and-resize the cleaned, background-knocked infinity raster to the same shared uniform 4:3 gallery canvas as the hero (reframe/resize only — no invented pixels).
Vectorize the second cleaned pool into a matching flat-color SVG.
Load the third client photo (kidney-shaped pool) to complete the set.
Finalize the kidney-pool upload for its prep chain.
Recover blown highlights from the hard midday sun so the water/plaster detail survives the trace.
Prompt-select the lawn/fence background to isolate the kidney pool and brick coping.
Solid-white knockout of the backyard so the trace stays clean at the pool edges.
Unify the light-blue plaster and brick-red coping into clean flat colors matching the gallery look.
Crop-and-resize the cleaned, background-knocked kidney raster to the same shared uniform 4:3 gallery canvas (reframe/resize only — no invented pixels).
Vectorize the third cleaned pool, completing the three-piece flat-color SVG set.

Export the hero's uniform-canvas cleaned raster to a flat-white-background web PNG at the shared gallery size (outputFileType=png, same 4:3 canvas — format-convert/resize only, no invented pixels). The background was knocked out to solid white earlier (step 7), so this PNG carries an opaque flat-white field, not alpha; it is the drop-in raster twin of the SVG.

Export the infinity pool's uniform-canvas raster to a matching flat-white-background web PNG at the same gallery size (outputFileType=png, same 4:3 canvas). Its background was solid-white-knocked at step 17, so the PNG carries an opaque flat-white field, not alpha; keeps the raster set uniform.

Export the kidney pool's uniform-canvas raster to the matching flat-white-background web PNG at the same gallery size (outputFileType=png, same 4:3 canvas). Its background was solid-white-knocked at step 25, so the PNG carries an opaque flat-white field, not alpha; completes the three-PNG web set for pool_vectors_web_png_set.zip.

connector_workflow.inputs_from: lagoon_vector_svg + infinity_vector_svg + kidney_vector_svg + lagoon_web_png + infinity_web_png + kidney_web_png
connector_workflow.note: Create the delivery project folder that will hold the three finished pool vectors and their matching web PNGs for the website handoff.

connector_workflow.inputs_from: lagoon_vector_svg + infinity_vector_svg + kidney_vector_svg + lagoon_web_png + infinity_web_png + kidney_web_png + pool_project_folder
connector_workflow.note: Collect the three vector outputs and their three matching web PNGs into the delivery folder, yielding the asset URNs needed for the moodboard and the PNG set.
Assemble the three finished pool-vector asset URNs onto a single Firefly board (deep-link) so the client approves the gallery set in one view before it ships to the website.

chaining_note: Strict output→input chaining throughout. The hero (freeform lagoon) defines the full recipe: upload(1)→finalize(2)→auto_straighten(3)→crop_and_resize(4)→select_subject(5)→invert_selection(6)→fill_area(7, consumes the cropped raster + inverted bg mask)→auto_tone(8)→brightness_and_contrast(9)→adjust_hsl(10)→crop_and_resize(11, uniform 4:3 canvas)→vectorize(12). Every step consumes the immediately prior step's output (and fill_area consumes both the cropped raster from step 4 and the inverted mask from step 6). The second pool reuses the recipe with framing-appropriate variations: upload(13)→finalize(14)→auto_tone(15)→select_by_prompt bg(16)→fill_area(17, raster from 15 + mask from 16)→adjust_hsl(18)→crop_and_resize(19, same uniform 4:3 canvas)→vectorize(20). The third pool: upload(21)→finalize(22)→adjust_highlights(23)→select_by_prompt bg(24)→fill_area(25, raster from 23 + mask from 24)→adjust_hsl(26)→crop_and_resize(27, same uniform 4:3 canvas)→vectorize(28). The three uniform-canvas cleaned rasters (11, 19, 27) are then each exported to a flat-white-background web PNG at the shared gallery size via crop_and_resize with outputFileType=png (29 from lagoon_uniform_canvas, 30 from infinity_uniform_canvas, 31 from kidney_uniform_canvas) — same 4:3 canvas, format-convert/resize only, no invented pixels; the backgrounds were solid-white-knocked (steps 7/17/25) and the chain has no alpha step, so these PNGs carry an opaque flat-white field, not transparency — producing the three-file web-PNG set. The three vectorize outputs (12, 20, 28) and the three web PNGs (29, 30, 31) then feed asset_create_folders(32)→asset_copy_assets(33, collects all three SVGs and all three PNGs into the folder)→create_firefly_board(34, assembles the collected SVG URNs into the approval moodboard). Nothing is generated from scratch: image_crop_and_resize only reframes/exports the cleaned raster to a uniform aspect and format (no invented pixels), image_fill_area only lays a solid color into the inverted background selection, and image_vectorize is the literal 'redraw as vector' deliverable. No Canva, no Express widget track, no local steps.
difficulty_rationale: Long-horizon and wide-mix: 34 connector calls and 16 distinct Adobe tools, well past the ≥10 / ≥6 bar, spanning three groups (asset I/O, photo/raster edit, board). It is genuinely hard because each of the three photos has a different geometry, palette, and defect profile (tilt+clutter; uneven exposure+vanishing edge; blown highlights+leaves), so the agent must adapt the isolation and normalization sub-chain per image rather than blindly replaying one macro — choosing select_subject vs select_by_prompt, and auto_tone vs highlights recovery, based on the shot. The strict requirement that fill_area receive BOTH a raster and the correct inverted/prompted mask, and that vectorize only succeed on a properly flattened, color-normalized, background-knocked raster, makes the ordering load-bearing: skip the tonal flatten or the HSL normalization and the trace degrades into noisy paths, failing the 'clean, detailed, visually appealing' bar. Everything stays inside the v2.1 capability sheet — image_fill_area is solid-only, image_crop_and_resize is reframe-only, image_vectorize is the core deliverable — with no generative fill, object removal, compositing, reframe-crop, or from-scratch illustration.

reverify.banned_capability_check: ZERO banned capabilities. no canvas-generation tool appears in connector_workflow or tools_used (0 occurrences). No text-to-image, no generative fill, no upscale/super-resolution, no AI object-removal-with-reconstruction, no compositing, no from-scratch illustration. image_fill_area (steps 7,17,25) is solid-color knockout only; image_crop_and_resize (steps 4,11,19,27 reframe; steps 29,30,31 same-canvas resize+PNG format-convert) is reframe/resize/format-convert only — never grows a dimension beyond the source and invents no pixels; image_vectorize (12,20,28) is the literal raster→SVG deliverable. The PNG exports carry an opaque flat-white background (solid-white knockout, no alpha step), not transparency. All 34 steps server=='adobe'.
reverify.notes: Fixed the outputs-vs-workflow completeness gap: pool_vectors_web_png_set.zip is now actually produced. Added explicit uniform-canvas crop_and_resize steps for pools 2 and 3 (19, 27) mirroring the hero's step 11 so all three share the identical 4:3 gallery canvas, then three PNG export steps (29 lagoon, 30 infinity, 31 kidney) via image_crop_and_resize with outputFileType=png — a real connector, gen-free, already in the tool set — sourcing the three uniform-canvas cleaned rasters. Folder/copy/board renumbered to 32/33/34 and their inputs_from extended to also carry the three web PNGs. tool_call_count 29→34; distinct_adobe_tools stays 16 (image_crop_and_resize already counted). tools_used unchanged (all new steps reuse already-listed tools). QA-fix pass: resolved the transparent-background contradiction — because the backgrounds are knocked out to solid white via image_fill_area (steps 7/17/25) and there is no image_remove_background/alpha step, the exported PNGs and the SVGs vectorized from those rasters carry an opaque flat-white backing field, not transparency. Relabeled the PNG-set output, the three SVG output specs, steps 29-31, and the one_line_ask / full_brief / grounding_note / chaining_note prose from 'transparent-background' to 'flat-white-background', and removed the misleading 'pad-to-canvas' phrasing (the source raster is already at the 4:3 uniform canvas, so the export is same-canvas format-convert/resize only). JSON re-validated; every step chains to a declared prior output or a declared input; all count assertions pass.
